messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw:
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"Look, I spend time online, you know? Just checking out pictures or maybe looking up some spots for a date or just something cool to photograph. But lately, it feels like everything is kinda mixed up—you see these stories that look real, but they're totally bogus, and it gets under your skin. It’s frustrating trying to figure out what’s true when half of it seems made up. For me, dealing with all this fake stuff isn't about getting super smart or knowing every single deep-dive fact; it’s more about slowing down and thinking before I believe something. When I see a picture or a post that makes my stomach drop—something really shocking, like maybe some crazy political thing or even just someone saying they found an instant cure for everything—I gotta stop myself right there. Instead of hitting the share button out of shock, I ask myself a few simple questions. First, who posted it? Are they someone I actually know, or is it some anonymous account with zero history? Second, where did they get this info? Is there another place, maybe a couple of different news sites or even just a reliable local source, that is saying the same thing? If it’s only popping up on one weird corner of the internet, chances are, man, it ain't true. My head says I need to be careful with my assumptions; jumping to conclusions is how you get burned. Also, when I look at photos, especially those dramatic ones for a painting or photography inspiration, I learn that sometimes they crop things or use filters that change the whole vibe. It’s not always what it looks like on the screen. The best way I've found is treating social media less like a news source and more like a gallery—something to look at, but not necessarily something to trust with your truth. If something makes you feel really angry or overly emotional right away, that's usually when you should pause because emotions are what these fake posts want from you. I try to remember that being thoughtful about the information is almost as important as doing nothing at all. It keeps me cool and helps me keep my focus on real life stuff, like hanging out with people or just taking a good walk through the city.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: Look, I spend time online, you know? Just checking out pictures or maybe looking up some spots for a date or just something cool to photograph. But lately, it feels like everything is kinda mixed up—you see these stories that look real, but they're totally bogus, and it gets under your skin. It’s frustrating trying to figure out what’s true when half of it seems made up. For me, dealing with all this fake stuff isn't about getting super smart or knowing every single deep-dive fact; it’s more about slowing down and thinking before I believe something. When I see a picture or a post that makes my stomach drop—something really shocking, like maybe some crazy political thing or even just someone saying they found an instant cure for everything—I gotta stop myself right there. Instead of hitting the share button out of shock, I ask myself a few simple questions. First, who posted it? Are they someone I actually know, or is it some anonymous account with zero history? Second, where did they get this info? Is there another place, maybe a couple of different news sites or even just a reliable local source, that is saying the same thing? If it’s only popping up on one weird corner of the internet, chances are, man, it ain't true. My head says I need to be careful with my assumptions; jumping to conclusions is how you get burned. Also, when I look at photos, especially those dramatic ones for a painting or photography inspiration, I learn that sometimes they crop things or use filters that change the whole vibe. It’s not always what it looks like on the screen. The best way I've found is treating social media less like a news source and more like a gallery—something to look at, but not necessarily something to trust with your truth. If something makes you feel really angry or overly emotional right away, that's usually when you should pause because emotions are what these fake posts want from you. I try to remember that being thoughtful about the information is almost as important as doing nothing at all. It keeps me cool and helps me keep my focus on real life stuff, like hanging out with people or just taking a good walk through the city.
